policy_statement: Utilize, maintain, and expand open space and natural areas along creeks for stormwater conveyance while meeting recreation, education, and transportation needs.
mechanism_description: The policy uses, maintains, and expands natural areas along creeks to convey stormwater, lowering flood and stormwater impacts while also supporting recreation, education, and transportation uses.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall/flash flooding) → nature-based stormwater conveyance/storage → ↓ runoff peak/overflow → ↓ exposure & damage (↑ stormwater resilience)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Land-use and stormwater management shifts toward preserving/restoring/expanding natural creek corridors, riparian open space, and floodplains (instead of—or in addition to—pipes/channels), integrating these areas with recreation/transport functions.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased infiltration and temporary storage, roughness/flow attenuation in vegetated corridors, reconnection of floodplains, and expanded conveyance capacity that reduces peak flows and erosive velocities.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced frequency/severity of flooding, sewer/stormwater overflows, and associated infrastructure/property damages under more intense precipitation regimes (improved resilience/adaptation outcomes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood and stormwater impacts (lower exposure/vulnerability) during extreme precipitation events via expanded natural stormwater conveyance and storage.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing a climate-amplified hazard (heavy rain/flooding) by increasing natural conveyance/storage to reduce flood impacts—an exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although ecosystem enhancement can yield carbon and heat-mitigation co-benefits, the stated intent and primary effect is flood risk reduction and stormwater resilience, which is adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a project were primarily justified/quantified for carbon sequestration or major energy/GHG reductions (rare for stormwater conveyance corridors), the classification could shift; likewise, if it were mainly a habitat restoration/carbon sink initiative it might be NBS-dominant. Under this mechanism definition (stormwater conveyance → flood risk reduction), adaptation remains dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy expanding natural stormwater conveyance to reduce flooding is principally reducing climate risk from precipitation extremes, regardless of location or co-benefits.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any finer tagging (e.g., “Nature-based adaptation,” “flood management,” “green infrastructure”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level review.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be alternatively labeled as Nature-Based Solutions in frameworks where “nature-based adaptation” is grouped under NBS; however, by dominant climate effect (flood risk reduction/resilience), Adaptation is the consistent primary category here.
key_indicators: "Utilize, maintain, and expand upon open space and natural areas along creeks"; "to convey stormwater"; "Complete Creeks Typology"
co_benefits: Recreation, education, transportation/active mobility, habitat/open-space amenities
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented through capital projects or land-use regulation, but the text reads as a general policy/strategy (no explicit mandate, standard, or funding commitment).
